title: Cacık
subtitle: Turkish yoghurt and cucumber dip, thinned to a sauce with cold water.
excerpt: Cacık is the Turkish cousin of tzatziki, lighter and more liquid, eaten both as a cold mezze dip and spooned alongside grilled meats as a sauce. Grated cucumber, yoghurt, garlic, dried mint, and olive oil come together in five minutes. Thin it with cold water and it doubles as a cold soup.
sourceNotes: A standard preparation across Turkish domestic cookery. The dip appears in Ottoman culinary manuscripts under variants of the name. Proportions and the cold-water thinning method follow the traditional Anatolian approach documented across twentieth-century Turkish food writing.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to a day ahead and keep covered in the fridge. Stir before serving; drain off any liquid that has separated from the cucumber.

content.text: Cacık is lighter than tzatziki. Where the Greek version is a thick dip, cacık is thinned with cold water to almost a sauce consistency. It appears alongside grilled kebabs and rice dishes throughout Turkey, spooned over meat rather than scooped up with bread. In summer it is thinned further still and served as a cold soup, a bowl of pale green yoghurt studded with cucumber, eaten with a spoon.

The method is quick and requires no cooking. Grate the cucumber coarsely and squeeze it in your fist over the sink; cucumber holds a lot of water and if you skip this step the finished dip will be watery inside thirty minutes. Combine with the yoghurt, garlic, mint, olive oil, and a pinch of salt, then add cold water tablespoon by tablespoon until you reach the consistency you want. Chill for at least thirty minutes before serving, which is enough time for the garlic to mellow and the flavours to come together.

items.prepNote: coarsely grated, excess liquid squeezed out

content.text: Grate {{cucumber}} cucumber on the coarse side of the box grater directly into a clean cloth or your hands over the sink. Squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible. Transfer to a mixing bowl.

Add {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{mint}} of dried mint, {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, and a good pinch of salt. Stir together. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, stirring between each addition, until the cacık reaches a consistency between thick dip and pourable sauce. Taste and adjust salt.

Cover and refrigerate for at least thirty minutes. Serve in a bowl with a drizzle of olive oil and a little extra dried mint crumbled on top. Eat alongside grilled meats, with flatbread, or as a cold soup on a warm day.

items.symptom: The cacık is watery after a short time
items.cause: The cucumber was not squeezed thoroughly enough.
items.fix: Next time, grate the cucumber earlier and leave it in a sieve with a pinch of salt for ten minutes before squeezing. This draws out more liquid.

items.cause: The garlic was too prominent, particularly if the dip was served immediately.
items.fix: Halve the garlic next time, or allow two hours of chilling rather than thirty minutes so the sharpness mellows.
Stir in cold water a tablespoon at a time until you reach the consistency you want.

content.text: Cacık turns up across the breadth of Turkish cooking: on the mezze table alongside olives, white cheese, and roasted peppers; beside the rice and lentil dishes of everyday home cooking; and, in its thinnest form, as a summer soup. It travels well, which is why versions of it appear from the Balkans through to Iran under different names. The dried-mint version is distinctly Turkish; Greek tzatziki uses fresh dill or mint and is considerably thicker.
